A History of Mind
Home
Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Nineteenth Century

Charles Darwin

1809 – 1882

Evolution, expression, and continuity between animal and human minds.

The central result is this: Darwin never wrote a formal philosophy of mind, but his work contains the materials for a remarkably coherent one. It is an evolutionary, embodied, comparative, affective, social, and theologically disruptive philosophy of mind. It does not simply “deny the soul” in a crude sense. Rather, it transfers most of the soul’s traditional explanatory work—reason, conscience, selfhood, religious feeling, language, emotion, moral obligation, even the sense of God—into a natural history of organisms, brains, instincts, habits, social life, and inherited dispositions. Darwin’s position is strongest when described not as simple atheistic materialism, but as an agnostic evolutionary naturalization of mind.

Darwin himself announced the program in the closing pages of On the Origin of Species: once species are understood through descent and modification, “psychology” will rest on a new foundation, namely the gradual acquisition of every mental power and capacity. In the same passage, he still speaks of natural laws as impressed on matter by the Creator, which means that the Darwinian revolution in mind begins not as a flat dismissal of theology but as a shift from special creation to law-governed secondary causes. In The Descent of Man, the program becomes explicit: Darwin argues that there is no fundamental mental difference in kind between human beings and higher mammals, however immense the difference in degree.

1. Preliminary caution: Darwin’s “philosophy of mind” is distributed, developmental, and partly private

A rigorous reconstruction has to distinguish at least four Darwins.

First, there is the private notebook Darwin of the late 1830s and early 1840s, who thinks boldly and often materialistically about mind, brain, religion, causation, free will, and conscience. In Notebook N, for example, he writes that “mind is function of body,” and in Notebook C he asks why thought as a secretion or property of the brain should be more wonderful than gravity as a property of matter. These are not polished doctrines, but they are philosophically decisive because they show what Darwin thought evolution threatened or transformed.

Second, there is the public scientific Darwin of Origin, Descent, and Expression. He is more cautious, but the philosophical movement is still clear: mental powers, instincts, emotions, moral sense, religious belief, and expression are to be treated as natural-historical phenomena.

Third, there is the autobiographical and epistolary Darwin, especially on religion. The Darwin Correspondence Project notes that Darwin was publicly reserved on religion and that the Autobiography is not a neutral, complete statement of his religious life; his correspondence is often more revealing. It also summarizes his final religious position as genuinely unsettled: his beliefs changed, never fixed into a simple doctrine, and his agnosticism was a real uncertainty about the existence and nature of God.

Fourth, there is “Darwinism” as later reception. Darwin’s work was appropriated by secularists, materialists, anti-materialists, social theorists, theologians, and polemicists in different national contexts. Darwin himself must not be flattened into Haeckel’s monism, Spencerian social Darwinism, Wallace’s spiritualism, or modern eliminative materialism. The source base supports a subtler claim: Darwin naturalizes the mind while remaining metaphysically cautious about ultimate questions.

2. The basic Darwinian transformation: psychology becomes genealogy

Darwin’s most important philosophical move is to make psychology genealogical. A mental faculty is not first defined by introspection, metaphysical essence, or divine endowment; it is investigated by asking how it could arise gradually in living organisms.

That move transforms the philosophy of mind. In a pre-Darwinian frame, “mind” often marks the point where nature stops and spirit begins. In Darwin’s frame, mind becomes one more domain of descent, variation, inheritance, adaptation, habit, and social life. The question is no longer, “What immaterial faculty must be posited to explain reason, morality, or religious belief?” It becomes, “By what gradations could capacities resembling these appear in animals and then reach human complexity?”

This is why Origin’s brief promise about psychology is so important. Darwin does not merely add evolution to biology; he extends the logic of descent to the “mental powers and capacities.” In Descent, he then argues directly that human mental faculties are continuous with animal faculties. The difference between a low fish and a high ape, Darwin says, is wider than the difference between a high ape and a human being, and the apparent gaps are filled by gradations.

Theologically, this is more radical than a simple argument against design. Darwin does not merely deny that God made animals. He deprives the human mind of its status as an obvious exception to natural history. If there is a soul, it can no longer be invoked lazily to explain everything difficult about human beings. Reason, conscience, self-consciousness, language, shame, prayer, superstition, sympathy, and devotion all become things with a history.

3. Continuity without identity: human beings are exceptional, but not metaphysically isolated

A common misunderstanding is that Darwin reduces human beings to “mere animals.” That is not quite right. Darwin’s claim is not that humans and other animals are mentally identical. His claim is that the differences are differences of degree, development, organization, and complexity rather than absolute metaphysical ruptures.

In Descent, Darwin writes that his object is to show that there is no fundamental difference between human beings and higher mammals in their mental faculties. He explicitly includes memory, attention, curiosity, imitation, reason, emotion, and social feeling among the faculties found, in less developed forms, outside humanity. He also treats self-consciousness, abstraction, and language not as supernatural insertions but as capacities that may emerge incidentally from high intellectual development, especially when combined with language.

The philosophical form of the claim is this: continuity is compatible with emergence. Darwin’s continuity thesis does not erase novelty. It says that novelty need not be miraculous. Human consciousness, morality, and religion can be unprecedented without being uncaused by organic history.

This is also where Darwin differs from many crude reductionisms. A Darwinian mind is not “nothing but” animal reflex. It is animal life become historically, socially, linguistically, and affectively complex. Darwin’s most famous mental formula is not “humans are beasts,” but something closer to: the human mind is an elaborated animal mind, transformed by social instincts, memory, habit, language, comparison, and inherited organization.

4. Mind and body: Darwin’s embodied naturalism

Darwin’s private notebooks are the strongest evidence for his underlying mind-body tendency. In Notebook N, he says that metaphysics should not try to solve mind by directly attacking “the citadel”; instead, a stable foundation must be built from natural history, and he writes bluntly that mind is a function of body. In Notebook M, he links passion, madness, bodily states, brain activity, and mental life, and he treats facts such as cold water changing one’s “spiritual frame” as arguments for materialism.

One especially revealing private note says that, to avoid stating too directly how far he believed in materialism, Darwin could say that emotions, instincts, and talents are hereditary because the child’s brain resembles the parent stock. This is not a public doctrine of reductive materialism, but it shows that Darwin understood the implications of his theory. If dispositions of mind are inherited with bodily organization, then mind cannot be treated as a wholly independent immaterial substance acting from outside nature.

Yet Darwin should not be made into a modern eliminativist. He does not deny pain, pleasure, feeling, consciousness, or emotion. He takes them seriously as facts of animal and human life. His naturalism is embodied rather than eliminative. Mental life is real, but it is real as the life of organisms. The mind is not detached from the nervous system, the muscles, the viscera, inherited instincts, expressive habits, and social relations.

That is the first major thesis of a Darwinian philosophy of mind:

Mind is not an exception to nature. It is a function, achievement, and expression of organized living bodies.

5. Instinct, reason, habit, and association: the architecture of Darwinian mind

Darwin’s philosophy of mind cannot be understood without instinct. In Origin, he refuses to define instinct sharply, but he treats it as action that may occur without prior experience and without knowledge of the purpose served. He also notes that even “low” animals may show a small element of judgment or reason. That ambiguity is crucial. Darwin is not dividing the world into mechanical animal instinct and human reason. He is dissolving that opposition.

In the same chapter, Darwin argues that instincts vary, that mental qualities in domestic animals vary, and that such variations can be inherited and selected. This makes instinct a plastic historical phenomenon rather than a fixed divine program. In the notebooks, he repeatedly treats the boundary between reason and instinct as gradual, not absolute. Notebook C explicitly suggests that reason and instinct are separated by a hiatus, not a saltus—that is, by a gap that can be historically bridged, not by a metaphysical leap.

Habit is the second major mechanism. In The Expression of the Emotions in Man and Animals, Darwin explains expression through serviceable associated habits, antithesis, and the direct action of the nervous system. Habits may become automatic, inherited, and expressive even when the original intention or use has faded. This is philosophically powerful because it turns much of mind into sedimented history. What looks like immediate expression may be an inherited trace of earlier use.

A French interpretation captures this well: Darwin shifts intentionality away from conscious meaning and toward organic history. Behavior may carry meaning now because it once had use, or because bodily systems discharge in regular ways, not because the organism consciously intends everything it expresses.

From this follows a second major Darwinian thesis:

Mental life is layered. Some of it is conscious and deliberative; much of it is habitual, inherited, affective, automatic, or socially acquired.

That thesis is deeply modern. Darwin is not Freud, nor a contemporary cognitive scientist, but he clearly breaks with any philosophy that identifies mind entirely with transparent self-conscious reflection.

6. Consciousness: real, graded, embodied, and not the whole of mind

Darwin does not offer a “hard problem of consciousness” theory in the later philosophical sense. He does not ask, in contemporary analytic vocabulary, why physical processes should have subjective phenomenal character. But he does have a view of consciousness, and it is more subtle than simple behaviorism.

First, consciousness is real. Darwin repeatedly discusses pleasure, pain, fear, joy, grief, shame, devotion, love, sympathy, and suffering in humans and animals. In his religious reflections, the suffering of sentient beings is one of his central arguments against a benevolent cosmic order. A thinker who makes animal suffering central is not denying consciousness; he is widening its moral and biological scope.

Second, consciousness is graded. Human self-consciousness, abstraction, and reflective thought are not present in the same way in all animals, but Darwin resists making them absolute metaphysical boundaries. In Descent, he suggests that self-consciousness and abstraction may arise as incidental results of highly developed faculties, especially language. In his notebooks, he explores “double consciousness,” memory, and the continuity of self across mental states, treating individuality as tied to consciousness reaching back to former periods.

Third, consciousness is not the whole of mind. Expression repeatedly emphasizes actions that may occur without conscious effort: reflexes, habitual movements, inherited expressive tendencies, and direct nervous discharges. Darwin says complex movements can be performed without the least effort or consciousness once they have become habitual. This means that consciousness, for Darwin, is not the sovereign author of all behavior. Much of the mind is beneath, behind, or before reflective awareness.

Fourth, self-consciousness is social. Darwin’s analysis of blushing is especially important. He calls blushing the most strictly human expression and connects it to attention to one’s appearance and to what others think. Blushing is not merely a bodily reaction; it is embodied social self-awareness. The self becomes conscious of itself under the imagined gaze of others.

A contemporary Spanish-language discussion of Darwinian consciousness, through John Searle, helps illuminate the modern philosophical stakes. Searle argues that both traditional dualism and reductive materialism go wrong when they fail to treat consciousness as a real biological feature of the world, caused by brain processes but not thereby unreal. That is not Darwin’s own formulation, but it is a plausible descendant of Darwin’s biological naturalization of mind: consciousness is neither supernatural vapor nor illusion; it is an organismic phenomenon.

A Darwinian philosophy of consciousness can therefore be stated as follows:

Consciousness is a real biological and social achievement of organisms, continuous with animal sentience, dependent on bodily organization, and only partly transparent to itself.

7. Emotion and expression: the body as the visible grammar of mind

Darwin’s Expression is one of the most important nineteenth-century works for philosophy of mind because it refuses to treat emotion as private inner vapor. Emotion appears in posture, muscle, skin, voice, circulation, reflex, habit, and communication.

Darwin’s three principles—serviceable associated habits, antithesis, and direct nervous action—allow him to explain why emotions are expressed bodily and why similar expressions occur across humans and other animals. He also argues that human races share many expressions, which supports common descent, and that laughter, fear, and other expressive forms have roots older than humanity itself.

This gives Darwin an embodied theory of affect. Emotions are not merely internal mental episodes that accidentally produce bodily signs. Bodily expression is part of how emotions exist, intensify, communicate, and become socially intelligible. Darwin says expression reveals thoughts and intentions, supports sympathy, and can even intensify emotion; repression can soften it.

A German-language summary of Darwin’s emotion theory rightly emphasizes that Darwin studied emotional expression phylogenetically: emotions are conscious states in humans and higher animals, expression is outward evidence of feeling, and expression also feeds back into feeling. This captures a crucial Darwinian point: affect is not an irrational leftover beneath mind; it is one of mind’s central evolutionary forms.

A Darwinian philosophy of emotion therefore says:

Emotion is embodied, expressive, communicative, inherited, historically layered, and continuous across species.

This has theological significance. If shame, awe, fear, dependence, devotion, gratitude, and hope all have bodily and evolutionary histories, then religious feeling can no longer be treated as an unmediated proof of a supernatural soul. It may still have theological meaning, but its occurrence is not self-authenticating evidence of divine origin.

8. Moral consciousness: conscience without a supernatural faculty

Darwin’s theory of conscience is one of the most philosophically important parts of Descent. He explicitly calls the moral sense or conscience the most important difference between human beings and animals, and he says that no earlier approach had treated it sufficiently from the side of natural history. His proposal is bold: any animal with well-marked social instincts would acquire something like a moral sense once its intellectual powers became sufficiently developed.

The basic mechanism is this. Social animals have durable social instincts: sympathy, attachment, concern for companions, discomfort at separation, and cooperative impulses. Momentary desires may override these instincts. But memory later recalls the action, compares the transient impulse with the more enduring social disposition, and produces dissatisfaction, remorse, or approval. Language, habit, social judgment, and inherited tendencies then stabilize this into conscience. In Descent, Darwin connects conscience to comparison between past conduct and social instincts, strengthened by habit and perhaps inheritance.

This means Darwin’s conscience is not a simple utilitarian calculator. It is not mere selfish prudence. Nor is it a Kantian noumenal faculty outside nature. It is a naturalized “ought” generated by the conflict between impulses, the persistence of social instincts, memory, sympathy, and reflective comparison.

Darwin had been moving toward this position in the notebooks. In Notebook N, he suggests that if an animal had reason plus social and sexual instincts, it would develop conscience, though its conscience might differ from ours if its instincts differed. That last clause is important: morality has natural roots, but its specific shape depends on the instincts, social form, and intellectual development of the organism.

Portuguese and Spanish discussions of Darwin’s moral thought are useful here because they often stress what Anglophone shorthand misses. A Portuguese study of Darwinian morality emphasizes that Darwin biologically grounds questions formerly treated as metaphysical or theological and that his theory of conscience depends on persistent social instincts, conflict among impulses, association, and habit. A Spanish-language study likewise describes Darwin’s moral philosophy as an account of the evolutionary origin of conscience, rooted in sympathy and sociability, while noting that sociability is necessary but not sufficient for full moral life.

This is where theological comments are essential rather than distracting. The doctrine of conscience has often been tied to the soul, divine law, or an implanted moral sense. Darwin does not merely reject that tradition from outside; he offers a rival genealogy of the same phenomenon. He explains why conscience feels authoritative without treating it as a direct supernatural voice. The feeling of obligation is real, but its reality is compatible with natural origin.

A Darwinian philosophy of moral mind can be summarized this way:

Conscience is the reflective, affective, socially formed memory of an animal whose social instincts have become intelligent.

That formula preserves both sides: morality is natural, but not trivial. It is animal in origin, but human in articulation. It is affective before it is theoretical, but reason and language profoundly reshape it.

9. Religion: Darwin naturalizes religious belief without treating it as irrelevant

Darwin’s treatment of religion is not an incidental aside. It is central to his philosophy of mind because religion gathers many of the highest and most contested mental capacities: agency attribution, causality, imagination, fear, hope, dependence, love, submission, moral judgment, ritual, and metaphysical speculation.

In Descent, Darwin treats belief in spiritual agencies as a natural development of human mental powers. Religious devotion, he says, is highly complex, involving love, submission, dependence, fear, reverence, gratitude, and hope. He even uses the relation between a dog and its master as an analogy for some of the emotional elements later elevated in religious consciousness. He also argues that once human beings possess high mental faculties, they naturally imagine unseen agencies, and that poor reasoning can generate fetishism, polytheism, and superstition; improvement requires reason, science, and broader knowledge.

This is not a throwaway secular sneer. Darwin is giving religion a place in natural history. Religious belief emerges from real cognitive and affective powers, but those powers do not guarantee the truth of their objects. The same faculties that make theology possible also make superstition possible.

His notebooks show the same pattern earlier and more sharply. In Notebook N, Darwin links the idea of deity to reason acting on causation and perhaps to conscience. In Notebook M, he describes a three-stage movement from divine will, to metaphysical abstractions, to laws, and speculates that will itself may arise from fixed laws of organization. In another note, he considers the “innate idea” of a Creator not as a separate divine act but perhaps as part of natural law and cultural development.

Here we reach one of Darwin’s deepest theological disruptions. Religion is not dismissed as nonsense. It is made explicable. Once explicable, it loses its privilege as immediate evidence of supernatural origin.

10. Darwin’s own theology: from Paley to agnosticism

Darwin’s early education was shaped by natural theology, especially the Paleyan idea that design in nature points to a designer. Origin does not begin as an anti-theological manifesto. Its final pages still speak of life under laws impressed on matter by the Creator and of the “grandeur” of life evolving through law. But the explanatory burden has shifted. Design no longer needs to be explained by direct contrivance; adaptation can arise through natural selection.

Darwin’s later autobiographical reflections show how far this shift went. He says that he once had a strong inward conviction of God and the immortality of the soul, especially under the sublimity of the Brazilian forest, but that such feelings eventually ceased to count for him as evidence. Feelings of sublimity, music, and awe could no longer bear theological proof.

The problem of suffering also weighed heavily on him. Darwin argued that the suffering of sentient beings is difficult to reconcile with an intelligent benevolent First Cause, whereas such suffering is intelligible under natural selection. This is not merely an ethical objection to theology. It is tied to his philosophy of mind: if animals are sentient, if pain is real, and if nature is full of non-moral suffering, then any theology of creation must account for animal consciousness and animal misery, not only human sin or human destiny.

Darwin’s final position is best described as agnostic. He says that the mystery of existence is insoluble to us and that he must be content to remain agnostic. He also worries that the human mind, evolved from a lower animal mind, may not be trustworthy when it reaches grand metaphysical conclusions. The Darwin Correspondence Project’s summary is consistent with this: his religious views changed and never settled into a fixed doctrinal position; his agnosticism was not a pose but an expression of genuine uncertainty.

This gives us a theological epistemology:

Human beings naturally form religious and metaphysical beliefs, but the evolutionary origin of our minds gives us reason to be cautious about their ultimate reliability.

That caution cuts both ways. It weakens dogmatic theology, but it also weakens dogmatic anti-theology. Darwin’s mature stance is not “science has proved there is no God”; it is closer to “science has explained much that theology formerly explained, while ultimate metaphysical questions remain beyond our secure powers.”

11. The soul: what Darwin displaces, what he leaves unresolved

Darwin’s thought about the soul must be handled carefully. There are at least three meanings of “soul” in play.

First, if “soul” means the ensemble of higher human capacities—reason, conscience, language, self-consciousness, moral feeling, religious aspiration—then Darwin naturalizes the soul. These capacities are not denied. They are given genealogies.

Second, if “soul” means an immaterial, immortal substance radically separate from animal life, Darwin’s work undermines its explanatory necessity. His continuity thesis, his animal psychology, his account of conscience, his theory of emotional expression, his natural history of religious belief, and his treatment of self-consciousness all make it increasingly difficult to use the soul as a scientific explanation. The soul becomes metaphysically possible only by becoming scientifically idle.

Third, if “soul” means an ultimate theological reality beyond scientific classification, Darwin does not conclusively refute it. In Descent, he allows that certain “spiritual powers” cannot be classified by the naturalist in the same way as bodily structures, even while he insists that mental faculties themselves do not differ in kind between humans and other animals. His own final agnosticism also leaves ultimate questions open, though not in the confident form traditional theology might want.

The notebooks are more radical. In Notebook M, Darwin comments on Plato’s argument for innate ideas and the preexistence of the soul by writing, in effect, “read monkeys” instead of preexistence. That compressed remark is devastating. It suggests that what philosophers had taken as evidence of a soul before birth might instead be explained by inherited animal ancestry. The “a priori” becomes phylogenetic.

So the most accurate conclusion is this:

Darwin does not present a formal disproof of the immortal soul. He performs an explanatory displacement of the soul.

That displacement is more philosophically serious than polemical denial. It says: wherever the soul was invoked to explain mind, ask first whether descent, embodiment, social instinct, nervous organization, habit, language, and inherited variation can explain the phenomenon. In case after case, Darwin thinks they can.

12. Free will: organization, law, and the weakening of libertarian agency

Darwin’s thoughts on free will are most explicit in the notebooks. In Notebook M, he speculates that will may arise from fixed laws of organization and that human willing may be as law-governed as other bodily events, even if experienced differently. He also considers a view in which free will, if found in oysters, animals, and humans alike, would be the direct effect of organization.

This does not produce a finished compatibilist theory, but it clearly undermines libertarian dualism. Will is not an uncaused spiritual power intervening in matter. It is an organismic power, continuous across life, shaped by instinct, habit, passion, and bodily organization.

That matters for conscience. If conscience is naturalized, moral responsibility cannot depend on a metaphysically unconditioned will. It must depend on the organism’s capacity for memory, anticipation, sympathy, instruction, social habit, and self-regulation. Darwin therefore shifts moral philosophy away from absolute metaphysical freedom and toward the development of character, social emotion, and reflective control.

13. Language, abstraction, and selfhood

Darwin treats language as transformative but not supernatural. In the notebooks, he links early language to association between things and voice, and in Descent he treats language as deeply connected with higher intellectual development. Language does not create mind from nothing; it reorganizes and amplifies pre-existing capacities.

This is crucial for self-consciousness. Darwin does not posit a Cartesian ego given in pure inwardness. The self emerges from memory, bodily continuity, social attention, language, and comparison. His reflections on double consciousness show that he was aware of fractures in selfhood: if memory and conscious continuity are interrupted, individuality becomes philosophically unstable.

Blushing again becomes decisive. It shows a self aware of itself as an object for others. The human self is not just a thinker; it is a social animal exposed to judgment.

Thus a Darwinian theory of selfhood would be:

The self is not a simple immaterial substance known prior to nature. It is the historically and socially stabilized consciousness of an embodied organism.

14. Darwin’s method: how to study mind scientifically

Darwin’s philosophy of mind is not only a set of theses; it is also a method. He studies mind through comparison, development, pathology, behavior, expression, domestication, children, cross-cultural reports, and animal observation.

In Expression, he uses evidence from animals, infants, different human populations, blind-born persons, and bodily reactions to reconstruct the history of emotional expression. He explicitly believes that animal facts can sometimes be less deceptive than human self-report, because animals do not theorize about their own emotions in the same way humans do.

This method is anti-introspectionist in a precise sense. It does not deny introspection; it refuses to make introspection sovereign. The mind is studied from the outside as well as the inside, through its phylogeny, ontogeny, bodily signs, social consequences, and pathological disruptions.

This is why Darwin’s famous notebook remark that understanding a baboon would do more for metaphysics than Locke is not a joke but a methodological revolution. The philosophy of mind must pass through animal psychology.

15. Non-Anglophone reception and interpretive debates

Several non-Anglophone discussions help broaden the reconstruction.

French-language work has often emphasized the fate of l’âme—the soul or mind—as it becomes annexed to nature after Darwin. One French historical discussion notes that by the late nineteenth century, the mind, long treated as the sign of rupture between humans and animals, was increasingly understood through biological evolution, child development, language, and technique. Another French analysis of Darwin and intentionality stresses that Darwin empties certain expressions of conscious intentional meaning by tracing them to habit, automatism, and organic causation. That is an important corrective to readings that make Darwin only a theorist of adaptation; he is also a theorist of de-intentionalized meaning.

French debates also help separate Darwin from “moral Darwinism.” A Cairn discussion notes that Darwin has often been vilified, misunderstood, or unread, and that his writings on moral emergence are not equivalent to later social-Darwinist moral ideology. This matters because Darwin’s naturalization of conscience does not imply that whatever survives is morally right.

Portuguese-language scholarship similarly emphasizes that Darwin brought moral and psychological questions into biology without thereby reducing them to crude egoism. A Portuguese study highlights Darwin’s shift from Paley’s future utility toward historical origin: moral rules and instincts are explained by their development, not by immediate theological design. A Portuguese interview with Jesuit philosopher Louis Caruana makes a related point from a theological angle: Darwin’s account of moral sentiments is important, but it does not by itself settle all moral philosophy, and eugenics cannot be justified simply by invoking natural selection.

Spanish-language discussion of Darwin and moral conscience likewise stresses sympathy, sociability, and the normative dimension of Darwin’s moral psychology. Spanish reception through Searle’s biological naturalism also shows one possible modern continuation: after Darwin, consciousness can be treated as fully biological without being dismissed as unreal.

German-language reception often brings Haeckel into view. Haeckel radicalized Darwin into a monistic worldview in which spirit and matter, body and soul, were unified and no creator was required. But that is Haeckel, not simply Darwin. The distinction is important. Darwin’s own texts naturalize mind, but his mature personal position is agnostic rather than programmatically monist.

Italian theological and scientific reception also keeps pressure on the unresolved question of consciousness. An Italian DISF discussion drawing on John Eccles presents Darwinian theory as a major scientific achievement while still treating the origin of human consciousness as an unresolved enigma and leaving room for human irreducibility. Whether one accepts Eccles’s conclusion or not, the debate clarifies the central tension: Darwin’s framework powerfully explains the development of mental capacities, but some philosophers and theologians still ask whether subjective consciousness itself has been fully explained.

The multilingual reception therefore reinforces the main interpretation: Darwin’s thought is not merely “materialism versus religion.” It is a field of tensions among naturalization, moral emergence, animal continuity, theology, consciousness, and the residual metaphysical question of the soul.

16. A reconstructed Darwinian philosophy of mind

The most comprehensive extraction from Darwin can be formulated as the following system. 1. Genealogical principle: every mental power should be investigated as something with a history. Reason, conscience, language, religion, and self-consciousness are not exempt from descent and development. 2. Continuity principle: human mental faculties differ from animal faculties primarily by degree, organization, and complexity, not by absolute kind. 3. Embodiment principle: mind depends on bodily and nervous organization. The brain, muscles, circulation, reflexes, inherited dispositions, and visceral states are not external to mental life; they are part of its conditions. 4. Affective priority: emotion, pain, pleasure, fear, attachment, sympathy, and social feeling are not secondary ornaments added to reason. They are basic elements in the evolution of mind. 5. Habitual and unconscious layering: much mental life is sedimented habit, inherited expression, automatic reaction, and nervous discharge. Conscious deliberation is important but not sovereign. 6. Social formation of mind: higher mind emerges in social animals. Sympathy, shame, conscience, imitation, communication, and concern for others are central, not peripheral. 7. Selfhood as social memory: the self is built from memory, continuity of consciousness, bodily life, language, and the imagined perspective of others. Blushing is a key sign of this social self-consciousness. 8. Moral naturalism without moral nihilism: conscience arises naturally from social instincts, memory, comparison, habit, and intelligence. This does not imply that morality is unreal; it means morality is an evolved feature of social mind. 9. Religion as natural mental product: belief in spiritual agencies and gods arises from ordinary human powers—causal reasoning, imagination, fear, dependence, devotion, and social emotion—rather than requiring a special supernatural faculty. 10. Soul displacement: the explanatory tasks traditionally assigned to the soul are progressively reassigned to natural history. The immortal soul is not formally disproved, but it becomes unnecessary for scientific explanation. 11. Theological agnosticism: Darwin’s science weakens Paleyan design and raises severe problems from animal suffering, but Darwin’s final position is not triumphant atheistic certainty. It is agnosticism grounded partly in the limits of an evolved mind. 12. Epistemic humility: because human reason itself has an evolutionary history, its grand metaphysical conclusions—whether theological or anti-theological—should be held with caution.

A concise label for this reconstructed view would be:

agnostic evolutionary psychophysical naturalism.

“Evolutionary” because mind develops through descent, variation, inheritance, selection, habit, and social life. “Psychophysical” because Darwin refuses to separate mental life from bodily organization. “Naturalism” because the explanatory work is done within nature. “Agnostic” because Darwin does not claim final metaphysical knowledge about God, immortality, or ultimate being.

17. What Darwin does not give us

Darwin does not give a complete philosophy of mind in the contemporary sense. He does not offer a detailed neural theory of consciousness, a formal theory of intentionality, a theory of qualia, or a developed account of semantic meaning. His views of inheritance include nineteenth-century assumptions about habit and acquired tendencies that later biology would revise. His writings also carry Victorian assumptions and language that require critical handling.

But these limits do not weaken the central achievement. Darwin changes the question. Before Darwin, the soul could be used as a stopping point: reason, conscience, religion, language, and selfhood were treated as signs that humanity stood outside animal nature. After Darwin, those same phenomena become research problems in the natural history of mind.

Conclusion

Darwin’s philosophy of mind is not a denial that humans think, feel, suffer, worship, deliberate, love, or judge. It is a denial that these powers must be explained by removing humans from nature. He makes mind continuous with life, consciousness continuous with animal sentience, conscience continuous with social instinct, religion continuous with imagination and causality, and selfhood continuous with memory, language, and social exposure.

The soul, in Darwin’s world, is not shouted down. It is made explanatorily unnecessary in most of the places where it had once seemed indispensable. Theology is not ignored; it is transformed into a question about the origin, authority, and reliability of human religious consciousness. The result is one of the most consequential philosophical shifts of the nineteenth century: the human mind becomes an evolved organ of life, and philosophy of mind becomes inseparable from biology, anthropology, animal behavior, emotion, morality, and the history of religion.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
